structured_choices.data_source: De-identified ICU EHR data from a multi-hospital system spanning 36 months. Data includes time-stamped vitals (heart rate, BP, SpO2, temperature, respiratory rate), laboratory results (lactate, creatinine, bilirubin, platelets, etc.), medication administrations, procedure codes, and demographics. Inclusion: all ICU admissions ≥18 years old with ≥4 hours of ICU stay. Exclusion: patients admitted solely for comfort care or with do-not-resuscitate orders in place at admission (to avoid label contamination from care-withdrawal decisions). Expected cohort size: 40,000–60,000 admissions.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label: in-hospital mortality within 72 hours of ICU admission. Death is defined as any mortality event occurring between ICU admission timestamp and 72 hours later, obtained from hospital discharge records. Patients discharged alive or still admitted at 72 hours are labeled negative. The prediction point is set at ICU admission; no data after +72 hours is used.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with site stratification. Training: admissions from months 1–24 across all hospital sites. Validation (for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping): admissions from months 25–30 across all sites. Test: admissions from months 31–36 across all sites, held out completely until final evaluation. Temporal split is chosen because (a) it prevents information leakage — the model cannot learn from future patterns it would not see in deployment; (b) it matches the realistic deployment scenario where the model is trained on historical data and deployed prospectively; (c) it avoids overfitting to site-specific or seasonal trends. Stratification by hospital site ensures the validation and test sets represent all participating institutions and accounts for site-level variation in mortality rates and documentation practices.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (imputation strategy, scaling/normalization, feature encoding) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24). The fitted transformers are then applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Specifically: (1) mean/median imputation for missing labs and vitals is computed from the training set distribution; (2) temporal normalization (e.g., z-score scaling of vital sign trends) uses training set statistics; (3) categorical encoding (e.g., procedure codes, medication classes) is derived from training set vocabulary. This prevents leakage of validation/test set distributional information into the model.
structured_choices.features: Two feature sets are constructed in parallel, one for each model. **Transformer model input (sequence representation):** Raw time-series data: vitals (HR, BP, SpO2, temp, RR) and lab results (lactate, creatinine, bilirubin, platelets, WBC, INR) are represented as irregularly sampled sequences. Missing values are imputed using forward-fill (within 6 hours) or mean imputation (beyond 6 hours). Each sequence is truncated or padded to a 72-hour window from ICU admission. Medication class (vasopressor, sedative, antibiotic, etc.) and procedure codes are embedded as binary or categorical features aligned to timestamps. Demographics (age, sex, admission type, reason for ICU admission) are concatenated as static features at the sequence start. **Gradient boosted model input (summary features):** Hand-engineered summaries computed over the first 24 hours post-admission: min/max/mean/trend of each vital and lab; count of procedures and medication administrations; presence of infection indicators (positive blood culture flag, antibiotic initiation); admission SOFA score (computed from available vitals and labs); age, sex, admission type. Both models receive no data after the 72-hour prediction window and no outcome-correlated administrative codes (e.g., palliative care flags).
structured_choices.model: **Primary model (Transformer):** A standard transformer encoder with 4 layers, 8 attention heads, hidden dimension 256, applied to the sequence of vital signs and lab results. A learned positional encoding handles irregular sampling. The sequence is pooled (mean or max over time steps) and passed through a 2-layer MLP (256 → 64 → 1 sigmoid output) for binary classification. Architecture is chosen because transformers can naturally handle irregular time series, learn complex temporal dependencies, and flexibly weight different features at different timepoints. **Baseline model (Gradient Boosted Trees):** XGBoost or LightGBM trained on the hand-engineered 24-hour summary features described above. Both are state-of-the-art in structured/tabular prediction and require no temporal modeling; this baseline represents the current standard in many clinical prediction settings. Justification for baseline: the same dataset, the same label, and equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (5-fold cross-validation on the training set, random search or Bayesian optimization over learning rate, tree depth, regularization strength) are applied to both models. The baseline is not reduced-dimensionality or untrained; it is a well-tuned, competitive model.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area under the receiver-operating-characteristic curve (AUROC). Justification: (1) Operationally, clinicians need to set an alert threshold; AUROC is threshold-independent and captures discrimination across all possible thresholds, showing whether the transformer model can better rank high-risk patients. (2) AUROC is appropriate despite class imbalance (mortality is typically 3–8% in ICU cohorts) because it does not collapse to trivial values when the negative class dominates, unlike accuracy. (3) AUROC is computed on the test set (months 31–36) for both models under identical conditions, ensuring a fair comparison. The hypothesis is considered **supported** if the transformer AUROC is ≥ 0.03 higher than the gradient-boosted baseline (a difference considered clinically meaningful in mortality prediction), with a 95% confidence interval not crossing zero.
secondary_metrics: Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC): more sensitive to performance on the rare positive class; prioritizes precision and recall trade-off.

Calibration (Hosmer–Lemeshow test and calibration plots): assesses whether predicted probabilities match true event rates, critical for clinical decision-making; poor calibration undermines the utility of the primary metric.
Sensitivity and specificity at the operating threshold chosen by the clinical team (e.g., 5% predicted probability): linked to actionability—too many false alarms reduce adoption.
Performance stratified by patient subgroups (age quartiles, admission type, sex): checks whether the transformer's advantage persists uniformly or is driven by specific populations.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping are performed on the validation set (months 25–30). For the transformer: validation AUROC is monitored during training; early stopping halts training if validation AUROC does not improve for 5 consecutive epochs. For the gradient-boosted baseline: 5-fold cross-validation is performed within the training set; hyperparameters are selected to maximize mean CV AUROC. Once hyperparameters are locked, both models are retrained on the combined training + validation data and evaluated exactly once on the held-out test set. This ensures the test set is not used for any tuning decision.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 31–36) is held out completely during all development, tuning, and validation phases. It is accessed only once, at the end, to compute the final AUROC and secondary metrics for both models. No test-set statistics (e.g., mean mortality rate, feature distributions) inform any modeling decision. Results are recorded and locked before any further analysis.
structured_choices.confound_controls: **Documentation intensity confound:** To test whether the transformer is capturing true physiologic signal or merely documentation patterns (e.g., more frequent vital monitoring in sicker patients is correlated with mortality but is not causal), the analysis includes an ablation check: a second transformer model is trained on the same data but with timestamps **randomly shuffled within each admission**. If the shuffled model retains >80% of the original AUROC, this suggests the model is learning from static feature presence rather than temporal order, pointing to documentation bias. A substantial drop in shuffled-model performance would support that temporal patterns are driving the advantage. **Feature-matched control:** The gradient-boosted baseline is given access to all 24-hour summaries *and* static embeddings of the sequence depth (e.g., number of vitals recorded, temporal density of labs) to ensure documentation intensity is available to both models. **Site-level validation:** Test-set AUROC is computed separately for each hospital site. If AUROC is >0.05 higher for some sites than others, and those sites have significantly different documentation or care protocols, this flags potential confounding; uniformity across sites strengthens causal interpretation. **Timestamp ablation:** A secondary experiment drops all timing information from the transformer (e.g., treats the sequence as a bag of features) to verify that temporal order, not just feature presence, drives the gain.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient Boosting for 72-Hour ICU Mortality Prediction

### 1. Data and Label Definition

We source de-identified ICU EHR data spanning 36 months from a multi-hospital system. The cohort includes all ICU admissions ≥18 years old with ≥4 hours of ICU stay; patients on comfort-care-only protocols at admission are excluded to avoid outcome contamination. Raw signals include time-stamped vitals (heart rate, blood pressure, SpO2, temperature, respiratory rate), labs (lactate, creatinine, bilirubin, platelets, WBC, INR), medication administrations (class and timing), procedure codes, and demographics. The label is binary: in-hospital mortality within 72 hours of ICU admission, extracted from discharge records. The prediction point is ICU admission; no information after +72 hours is used. Expected cohort size is 40,000–60,000 admissions.

### 2. Train/Validation/Test Split Strategy

Data are split temporally with site stratification. **Training set:** admissions from months 1–24 across all sites (~50% of data). **Validation set:** admissions from months 25–30 across all sites (~25% of data). **Test set:** admissions from months 31–36 across all sites (~25% of data), held completely out until final evaluation.

Temporal splitting is essential here. It prevents information leakage—the model cannot learn from future conditions it would not encounter at deployment time. It also reflects realistic deployment: we train on historical data and evaluate on prospective admissions. Temporal split is superior to random split because mortality and clinical practice evolve, and random mixing would contaminate the test set with training-era patterns. Site stratification ensures both validation and test sets represent all participating hospitals, guarding against site-specific confounds (e.g., documentation intensity, local protocols).

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Transformer model receives:** Irregularly sampled time-series data (vitals and labs) aligned to 72-hour windows from admission. Missing values are forward-filled within 6 hours or imputed with training-set means beyond 6 hours. Medications and procedures are encoded as binary/categorical features time-aligned to observations. Static features (age, sex, admission type, ICU reason) are appended at the sequence start. No data after 72 hours or outcome-correlated administrative codes (e.g., palliative care flags) are included.

**Gradient-boosted baseline receives:** Hand-engineered summaries over the first 24 hours: min, max, mean, and trend of each vital and lab; procedure and medication counts; infection indicators (blood culture positivity, antibiotic timing); SOFA score computed from available data; age, sex, admission type. This simulates the current clinical standard: structured feature engineering.

**Preprocessing fit point:** All imputation, scaling, and encoding transformers are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24) and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents leakage of future data distributions into training.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

**Transformer:** A 4-layer transformer encoder (8 attention heads, hidden dimension 256) with learned positional encoding to handle irregular sampling. Output is mean-pooled across the time dimension and passed through a 2-layer MLP (256 → 64 → 1, sigmoid) for binary classification. Transformers are chosen because they naturally capture irregular temporal dependencies and can weight different features dynamically.

**Baseline:** XGBoost or LightGBM on hand-engineered 24-hour summaries. This baseline is competitive and represents standard practice; it receives the same hyperparameter tuning effort as the transformer (5-fold cross-validation, random search or Bayesian optimization over learning rate, tree depth, L2 regularization). Both models are tuned on the same validation set to ensure fair comparison.

### 5. Primary and Secondary Metrics

**Primary metric:** AUROC (area under the receiver-operating-characteristic curve) on the test set. AUROC is chosen because (a) it is threshold-independent and shows discrimination across all operating points; (b) it is robust to class imbalance (mortality is typically 3–8%); (c) it directly measures whether the transformer can better rank high-risk patients—the operational goal. We declare the hypothesis supported if transformer AUROC exceeds the baseline by ≥0.03 with a 95% CI not crossing zero.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **AUPRC** (area under precision-recall curve): sensitive to rare positive class performance.
- **Calibration:** Hosmer–Lemeshow test and calibration plots ensure predicted probabilities match true rates.
- **Sensitivity/Specificity at clinical threshold:** e.g., at 5% predicted probability, how many high-risk patients are caught vs. how many false alarms?
- **Stratified performance:** AUROC, AUPRC, and calibration by age quartile, admission type, and sex to detect whether gains are universal or population-specific.

### 6. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Both models are tuned on the validation set (months 25–30). For the transformer, validation AUROC is monitored; early stopping halts training if AUROC plateaus for 5 consecutive epochs. For the baseline, 5-fold cross-validation within the training set selects hyperparameters. Once locked, both models are retrained on train + validation combined, then evaluated on the held-out test set exactly once. The test set is never touched during tuning.

### 7. Confound and Bias Controls

**Documentation intensity:** Sicker patients receive more frequent monitoring, which correlates with mortality but may not be causal. We run an ablation: train a transformer on the same data but with timestamps randomly shuffled within each admission. If this shuffled model retains >80% AUROC, the model is learning from feature presence, not temporal order—suggesting documentation bias. A >20% drop in AUROC supports temporal patterns as causal.

**Feature-matched baseline:** The gradient-boosted model receives documentative meta-features (e.g., number of vitals recorded, temporal density) alongside clinical features, ensuring both models have equal access to documentation signals.

**Site-level stratification:** Test-set AUROC is reported per site. Large variance signals site-specific confounding (e.g., differences in documentation standards or care protocols). Uniformity strengthens causal claims.

**Temporal ablation:** A secondary model removes all timing information (bag-of-features representation) to confirm temporal order drives the advantage.

### 8. Scope of Claims

This design tests whether a transformer on raw time-series data outperforms a well-tuned gradient-boosted model on hand-engineered summaries for 72-hour mortality prediction in a specific cohort and time window. It does **not** establish causality or clinical utility per se—that requires prospective deployment and outcome tracking. It also does not address external generalization beyond the held-out time window; temporal validity is tested but site-external validation would require separate data.
